title: Location-Invariant Properties of Functions Versus Properties of Distributions: United in Testing but Separated in Verification
gist: A FOCS 2026 paper by Oded Goldreich and Guy N. Rothblum (with Weizmann Institute, published via Apple ML Research) shows that although testing location-invariant properties of functions is tightly linked to testing the corresponding properties of distributions, this link breaks down for interactive proof-based verification, where functions admit efficient doubly-sublinear proofs but the analogous distribution properties provably do not.

insights.claim: For location-invariant (symmetric) properties, the query complexity of testing a function's property is closely tied to the sample complexity of testing the corresponding distribution property, but this equivalence does not carry over to verification via interactive proofs of proximity (IPPs).
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Signals a fundamental theoretical limit: techniques that unify function/distribution testing cannot be assumed to transfer to cheap verifiable-computation schemes, a caution for anyone building trust/verification layers on top of ML-adjacent statistical claims.
insights.quote: The main message of the current work is that this close relationship is not maintained in the context of verification.

insights.claim: The result holds under both general interactive proofs of proximity and the more restrictive doubly-sublinear IPPs (ds-IPPs), where the verifier's query complexity must be sublinear in testing complexity and the honest prover's query complexity must be sublinear in learning complexity.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Defines the precise efficiency bar (sublinear verifier and prover cost) that any practical 'lightweight verification' scheme for ML-scale data claims would need to clear.

themes.theme: Constructive results: functions are verifiable, distributions are not

insights.claim: The authors construct a doubly-sublinear IPP for functions from [m] to [n] where each value occurs exactly m/n times, achieving verifier query complexity O(n^{0.5-α}) and honest-prover query complexity e^{O(n^{0.5+α}/ε^2)} for any α in (0, 0.5).
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Concrete complexity bound showing verification of this uniform-occurrence function property can be done with roughly square-root-of-input-size query cost for the verifier.
insights.quote: the query complexity of the verifier is O(n^{0.5−α}), and the query complexity of the honest prover is e^{O(n^{0.5+α}/ε^2)}

insights.claim: A second doubly-sublinear IPP is constructed for functions where each value occurs either m/k times or not at all, with verifier complexity poly(1/ε)·k^{(2/3)-2α} and prover complexity poly(1/ε)·e^{O(k^{(2/3)+α})} for α in (0, 1/3).
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Second concrete efficiency benchmark for a natural sparsity-style property, extending the toolkit of verifiable function properties.

insights.claim: In contrast to the function-side results, prior work (Herman and Rothblum, 2025) shows the corresponding distribution properties have no doubly-efficient IPP at all, and the uniformity-over-[n] distribution property has no IPP where the verifier uses fewer than roughly sqrt(n) samples regardless of other complexity measures.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A hard negative result: cheap, sublinear-sample verification is provably impossible for basic distribution uniformity, meaning any product relying on distributional verification (e.g., checking a claimed data distribution cheaply) faces a real sample-complexity floor.
insights.quote: it is known that the corresponding properties of distributions have no doubly-efficient IPP
insights.locator: Contrast paragraph, 'In contrast, in both cases...'

themes.theme: Apple's investment in theoretical verifiable-computation research

insights.claim: This is a FOCS (Foundations of Computer Science) 2026 paper co-authored with Weizmann Institute of Science researchers, published on Apple's ML research site as part of a broader line of work on interactive proofs for verifying statistical/data-analysis claims without full replication.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Indicates Apple is funding or co-authoring top-tier theoretical CS research on verifiable computation, an area with long-run relevance to trustworthy/auditable AI systems rather than immediate product shipping.
